records.ety: [OE. summe, somme, OF. sume, some, F. somme, L. summa, fr. summus highest, a superlative from sub under. See Sub-, and cf. Supreme.]

defs: The aggregate of two or more numbers, magnitudes, quantities, or particulars; the amount or whole of any number of individuals or particulars added together; as, the sum of 5 and 7 is 12.
quotes: Take ye the sum of all the congregation.
notes: ☞ Sum is now commonly applied to an aggregate of numbers, and number to an aggregate of persons or things.

defs: A quantity of money or currency; any amount, indefinitely; as, a sum of money; a small sum, or a large sum.
quotes: With a great sum obtained I this freedom.

defs: The principal points or thoughts when viewed together; the amount; the substance; compendium; as, this is the sum of all the evidence in the case; this is the sum and substance of his objections.

quotes: Thus have I told thee all my state, and brought My story to the sum of earthly bliss.

defs: A problem to be solved, or an example to be wrought out.
quotes: A sum in arithmetic wherein a flaw discovered at a particular point is ipso facto fatal to the whole.
A large sheet of paper . . . covered with long sums.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Summed (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Summing.]

defs: To bring together into one whole; to collect into one amount; to cast up, as a column of figures; to ascertain the totality of; -- usually with up.
quotes: The mind doth value every moment, and then the hour doth rather sum up the moments, than divide the day.

defs: To bring or collect into a small compass; to comprise in a few words; to condense; -- usually with up.
quotes: “Go to the ant, thou sluggard,” in few words sums up the moral of this fable.
He sums their virtues in himself alone.

defs: To have (the feathers) full grown; to furnish with complete, or full-grown, plumage.
quotes: But feathered soon and fledge They summed their pens [wings].
